title: The Macro Minute: Is the US business cycle heating up?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's your skipper here, Darius Dale, to present our macro minute for Monday, June 1st, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead-off morning note. So, let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, is the US business cycle heating up? Our answer is yes. The highest headline print in the ISM Manufacturing PMI since May 2022 signals the US business cycle is gaining steam. Recall that the ISM Manufacturing PMI is a persistent leading indicator of the business cycle, meaning it leads both business cycle downturns and business cycle upturns. On April 14th, we concluded that dumb money bulls were going to leave smart money bears behind again for the fourth consecutive year. No change to the data-driven view. As always, we'll wrap up with a question from our community here in the 42 macro dashboard. This one's titled retail exposure to SpaceX. It says, "Hi DD and team 42. Thanks again for your work in helping us retire on time and comfortably." "Should retail investors do anything to avoid becoming a source of liquidity for SpaceX and other IPOs through their VT and other index fund exposure?" So, great question. So, the first thing I'll say is, unless you're going to not invest in the equity market, you will become a source of liquidity for all these IPOs, and that's the point that I'm making. The reason for that is IPOs are designed to create liquidity for insiders and venture capitalists, venture capital, you know, GPs and their LPs. Essentially, you're transferring units of of of equity for money from the public equity market. And so, you know, that's what an IPO is. You're you're raising liquidity from the public equity market. And so, unless you're not going to invest in these index funds or or in the IPOs themselves, you will be a source of liquidity. So, and and and you know, so obviously, you know, I had some some interesting tweets about SpaceX IPO this weekend, particularly in response to Cam Harvey, Professor Cam Harvey over at Duke, the Duke Business School. He had He put out an interesting, very interesting white paper about uh some of the dynamics around the SpaceX IPO and how quite frankly uh you know they're pretty um they're pretty questionable for lack of a better word uh with regards to uh how the IPO is being engineered in my opinion and again this is an opinion uh uh to create a bubble uh bubble-like response in the in the stock price post-IPO. Uh so for uh you know to to kind of start SpaceX itself is restricting uh the available float uh of the IPO to only 4% which is a like a historically small amount for a company of this size. You know talking about a near $2 trillion uh market cap. Uh so that's unusual in the context of most IPOs floating somewhere between 10 and 50% of the company. I mean 4% is hilariously low in that in that regard. Uh number two, you have the uh SEC was amending its disclosure rules in a way that allows these companies to uh Uh again in my opinion say whatever they want uh to drum up interest in these uh in these in in these in these offerings uh um obviously to accommodate SpaceX, Anthropic, and eventually OpenAI. Uh so that that's number two and then number three is probably the most uh interesting part of this uh whole process which is the index providers. S&P, Russell, Nasdaq, they are explicitly amending uh their index inclusion rules in a way that will pull forward the massive wave of passive demand for these securities. Typically for example I want to say uh don't quote me on this but I think either the S&P or the Nasdaq you typically have to be a profitable company for four four consecutive quarters in order to even be considered uh to be part of the index but obviously uh SpaceX which you know just recently lost $5 billion on a net income basis in the most recent year uh obviously is getting preferential treatment in terms of being able to be immediately applied to these indices. Uh you know so they're they're they're going to get preferential treatment Uh and I'm I'm my assumption is that uh Anthropic and Open AI are going to get preferential uh treatment uh as well. So, it essentially pulls forward all of that wave of passive uh buying uh that uh our friend Mike Green over at Simplify has outlined uh with his academic uh work on the subject matter. So, you have restricted float, restricting the supply the supply of the equity relative to the overwhelming uh demand, the accelerated the pulled forward demand. Uh at the same time, you have the SEC amending its rules in a way that is very favorable uh for each of these IPOs. So, the net result of all this is that the SpaceX IPO and the rest of the IPOs are probably going to bubble. They're trying to engineer very favorable response in the market uh to these uh to these IPOs. And so, you know, unless you just want to sit out the US equity market, uh you're going to be providing liquidity to these uh companies. But the whole goal is to uh participate in the capital appreciation uh uh um that that's that they're going to uh provide uh while at the same time being risk-aware enough to know that when it's time to get out uh heading into what we believe will be a secular bear market on the other side of this uh of this equity bull. Uh we think this equity bull transitions to a secular bear market, not a cyclical bear market that V-bottoms like all the bear markets that we've seen since the Great Financial Crisis. The secular bear market is one that doesn't V-bottom, that keeps going down and down and down for quarters if not years, uh that usually takes uh a long time to recover the high. I want to say the last two secular bear markets the last three secular bear markets that we've had uh took uh 5 and 1/2 years to recover the highs, 15 and 1/2 years to recover the highs, and then I think 4 and 1/2 years to recover the highs uh uh the previous highs. And so, that's that's the kind of outcome that we see as the most likely outcome. But between now and then, it's all about maximizing up earning in a risk-on market regime. So, your job in a risk-on market regime is to maximize upside capture. And when we transition to a risk-off market regime, which may or may not be the coming secular bear market, your job is to take down risk so you can minimize downside capture. And that's exactly what we do with KISS. That's exactly what we do with Dr. Mo. So, just wrapping up, uh I'll show you our uh Dr. Mo signals here. Obviously, the red bars indicate when Dr. Mo is bearish uh and short. Uh the gray bars indicate when Dr. Mo is uh has you no position. Light green bars indicate when Dr. Mo tells you to be a half position. And then the dark green bars tells you when Dr. Mo signals a long max position. And so as you can see here, Dr. Mo got back long a half position in tech back on you know, early April. And then I want to say on April 11th, 12th, 13th, got back to a long max position. I want to say tech is up about 30% since then. So the same six system that gets us out to prevent left tail risk in markets is the same exact system that puts us back in so that we can maximize upside capture in bull markets. Way too many investors out there, you know, overly focusing on the bear the negative side of things, keeping investors from making money. We don't keep you from making money in 42 macro. We help you make money and we help you keep it. And that's what KISS and Dr. Mo are designed to do. So we'll wrap it up there. Darius Dale here presenting our macro minute for Monday, June 1st, 2026. Best of luck out there today. We'll catch you back here tomorrow. Cheers.
